title: Paper Explained: Visual-ERM: Reward Modeling for Visual Equivalence - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: Turning Visuals into Accurate Code with Fine-Grained Rewards

content.background:
Imagine you’re teaching a student to recreate a chart, a table, or an SVG from a picture. In the past, people trained these models mainly with two types of feedback: word-based rules (describing what should be true in general) or rough visual similarity (the final image looks similar at a glance). Neither approach really checks the tiny, exact details that matter—like whether a bar’s height, a number in a cell, or a drawn line matches the ground truth exactly. That’s a big problem because even small mistakes can make the whole visual representation wrong.

When the feedback isn’t precise, the model has a hard time learning what “good” really means. It can end up taking shortcuts that look good at a high level but miss important details, a phenomenon scientists call reward hacking. In vision-to-code tasks, where the goal is to turn a precise image into correct code or structured output, those shortcuts prevent real progress. This misalignment between what the model is rewarded for and what we truly care about (pixel-perfect fidelity in the rendered visuals) keeps the accuracy bar stubbornly high and progress slow.

That’s why this line of research is needed. The idea is to create a reward signal that sits directly in the rendered visual space—so it can judge fidelity at the exact pixels and structures that matter, in a way that’s interpretable and usable across chart, table, and SVG tasks. Alongside this, new benchmarks are proposed to measure those fine-grained visual differences, helping us quantify true improvements instead of just shiny but flawed results. In short, this work aims to fix the feedback loop so models can learn to reproduce visuals accurately, not just look reasonably close.
content.methodology:
Here’s the core idea in plain terms and the main steps they took.

- What they’re trying to solve
  - In vision-to-code tasks, a model looks at a visual input (like a chart, table, or SVG) and emits code or a structured representation that should recreate that visual. Training with reinforcement learning needs a reward signal telling the model how good its rendering is. Traditional rewards are either hand-written rules or rough visual similarity, which miss fine details (e.g., exact axis placement or cell values) and can be gamed.
  - The key innovation is a visual-aware reward system, called Visual-ERM, that judges the quality of the model’s output by looking at the final rendered image itself, not just text rules or coarse features. Think of it as a visual critic that cares about the exact look of the end product.

- How Visual-ERM works, in simple steps
  - Step 1: A multimodal reward critic is built. This critic takes the original input image and the model’s produced rendered image (from the inferred code) and then spits out a reward. It’s “multimodal” because it processes visual information (images) and can relate it to the target structure, possibly using other signals like textual hints about the task, but crucially it judges in the visual space.
  - Step 2: It learns from examples. The system is trained on data where good visual reconstructions are known, so the critic learns to detect fine-grained differences—like a slight misalignment of a bar, a wrong value in a cell, or a misplaced axis label.
  - Step 3: The reward is fed into reinforcement learning. During training, the vision-to-code model proposes a rendering from the input image; Visual-ERM rates that rendering with a precise, interpretable reward; the model updates its behavior to maximize these rewards.
  - Step 4: The feedback is interpretable. Instead of a single vague score, Visual-ERM can indicate where the rendering diverges from the target visual, helping the model (or researchers) understand what to fix next.

- Why this matters and what they showed
  - Beyond a single number, the reward is grounded in the actual look of the final image, which aligns much more closely with the goal of vision-to-code tasks. This helps prevent reward hacking that happens when rewards don’t reflect visual fidelity.
  - They introduced a test-bed called VC-RewardBench to evaluate how well rewards capture fine-grained image-to-image differences in structured visuals. In experiments, Visual-ERM at 8B parameters outperformed much larger baselines and came close to leading closed-source models on this kind of task.
  - In practice, incorporating Visual-ERM into training yielded significant gains: about +8.4 on chart-to-code, and consistent improvements on table and SVG parsing (roughly +2.7 to +4.1 on average). They also explored at-test-time strategies like reflection and revision to further boost performance.

In short, the main idea is to replace coarse or textual rewards with a visual, interpretable reward signal that directly judges how faithfully the model’s rendered output matches the target visual. This visual feedback is learned as a flexible, task-agnostic critic, and it’s integrated into reinforcement learning to produce vision-to-code systems that are more accurate, robust, and easier to diagnose.
content.results:
This paper tackles a tricky goal: getting models to turn visual outputs like charts, tables, and SVG images into accurate, executable representations (vision-to-code). The key idea is a new reward model called Visual-ERM that can judge the quality of these vision-to-code results by looking at the actual rendered visuals, not just by checking text rules or rough image similarity. In other words, instead of teaching the model with vague or easily fooled signals, Visual-ERM provides fine-grained, interpretable feedback that mirrors how the final visuals should look. This reward is then used to guide reinforcement learning, helping the model learn to produce code that faithfully recreates the input visuals.

Compared to previous methods, Visual-ERM avoids two common pitfalls: reward signals based on hand-written rules or coarse, high-level similarity guesses. Those approaches often miss small but important visual details and can be gamed or misaligned with the real task. By evaluating outputs directly in the rendered visual space, Visual-ERM gives the model precise guidance about where it misses the mark—down to subtle pixel-level differences in a chart or table. The researchers also show that this approach works across different types of structured visuals, not just one specific task, making it a more robust and general solution. They also add a test-time improvement mechanism (reflection and revision), letting the model rethink and refine its output, further boosting reliability.

A practical contribution of the work is a new benchmark, VC-RewardBench, designed to probe how well models detect fine-grained visual discrepancies in structured visuals. On this benchmark, the Visual-ERM approach holds its own against very large, openly available models and approaches the performance of leading closed-source systems. Taken together, the work suggests a powerful, general recipe for vision-to-code RL: give the model a fine-grained, visually aligned reward signal, and it can learn more accurate, robust reconstructions across charts, tables, and SVGs without relying on task-specific hand-tuning. This could make automated data visualization, reporting, and analysis tools more reliable and easier to scale in real-world applications.
content.significance:
Visual-ERM tackles a core bottleneck in multimodal AI: how to give a model precise, trustworthy feedback when it has to turn visuals (like charts, tables, and SVGs) into structured outputs or code. Traditional rewards for vision-to-code tasks often rely on rough textual checks or generic visual similarity, which miss fine-grained differences and can be gamed. Visual-ERM changes the game by using a multimodal reward model that operates in the rendered visual space—essentially judging how close the produced visual result is to the ground truth, in a way that’s interpretable and task-agnostic. This lets the training loop reward the model for pixel-accurate, layout-consistent reconstructions, which translates into much better performance on chart-to-code, table parsing, and SVG tasks (for example, the paper reports strong gains on these benchmarks when integrated into RL).

In the long run, this idea is likely to influence how we train and evaluate multimodal systems beyond vision-to-code. By focusing rewards on fine-grained visual fidelity rather than coarse proxies, researchers can build more robust agents that generate, edit, and verify complex visual outputs—think live dashboards, data visualizations, or design tools that translate visuals back into executable formats. The introduction of VC-RewardBench as a benchmark for image-to-image discrepancies helps standardize how we measure subtle visual correctness, encouraging broader adoption of similar high-fidelity reward signals. This approach resonates with the broader trend in AI of moving from text-only alignment (as in many chat-based systems) toward multimodal alignment where the feedback itself can be grounded in the actual perceptual output. For modern systems like ChatGPT and other vision-enabled assistants, Visual-ERM-inspired ideas suggest a path to more reliable, verifiable results when users provide or request visual data—reducing reward hacking and making model improvements more directly tied to the quality of the final visual output.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Visual Equivalence Reward Model: The Heart of Visual-ERM
concept_explanation.content:
Think of Visual-ERM like a very detailed art critic for AI drawings. If you ask a student to recreate a chart from data, the critic doesn’t just say “good job.” It points out precise differences: “the bar on the left is a bit shorter, the axis labels are off, the colors don’t match, and the legend is missing.” That kind of fine-grained feedback helps the student fix specific parts of the drawing. Visual-ERM does something similar, but for AI systems that translate a visual input (like a chart, table, or SVG) into code that can render that image. It compares the target image with the model’s rendered image and gives a detailed, quantitative reward telling you how visually equivalent they are. The reward is designed to be interpretable and useful for guiding learning, not just a rough guess of similarity.

Here’s how it works, step by step. First, you have a vision-to-code task: the model reads some input (data or description) and writes code that renders a chart, a table, or an SVG. The model’s code produces a rendered image. Second, a separate, multimodal reward model called Visual-ERM looks at two things—the ground-truth image you want to match and the model’s rendered image—and judges how close they are in visuals. This judge isn’t limited to simple rules or coarse “how similar in words” checks; it’s trained to notice fine differences in details like bar heights, axis tick marks, label placement, color palettes, line thickness, and other subtle visual features. Third, the score from Visual-ERM is used as the reward signal in reinforcement learning to update the vision-to-code model, so the system learns to produce outputs that are visually faithful to the target.

To make the idea concrete, consider chart-to-code. Suppose the ground-truth chart shows three bars with specific heights, colors, and axis labels. The code generated by the model should recreate that chart exactly. If the leftmost bar is too short or the axis labels are missing, Visual-ERM detects those small, visual discrepancies and returns a lower reward; if the render matches closely, it returns a higher reward. The beauty of this approach is that the reward reflects actual image quality, not just textual cues or rough similarity. Visual-ERM is designed to be task-agnostic, meaning it can handle different kinds of visuals (charts, tables, SVGs) without requiring hand-crafted rules for each task.

This concept matters because reward signals in reinforcement learning can be brittle. If you rely on simple rules or coarse similarity, the model might “game” the reward without truly improving the visual quality (a problem researchers call reward hacking). Visual-ERM provides finer, more interpretable feedback directly in the visual space, helping the model learn to produce truly faithful visuals across multiple formats. The paper reports impressive improvements when using Visual-ERM: for a strong base model (Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct), chart-to-code gains of +8.4, and improvements on table and SVG parsing (+2.7 and +4.1 on average). They also introduce a benchmark, VisualCritic-RewardBench (VC-RewardBench), to test fine-grained image-to-image discrepancies, showing that Visual-ERM at 8B nearly matches or surpasses larger, closed-source models on this task. These results suggest that precise visual reward supervision is both necessary and effective for vision-to-code RL, regardless of the specific visual task.

In terms of practical use, Visual-ERM can power tools that automatically generate accurate visual representations from data, such as chart creators, dashboard generators, or SVG-based design tools. It enables developers and students to build systems that reliably recreate complex visuals from inputs, with the model learning from fine-grained visual feedback. Beyond charts, tables, and SVGs, this approach could extend to any task where the output is a visually rendered artifact and fidelity matters—think reproducible data visuals, QA checks for generated visuals, or interactive notebook assistants that translate data into precise visual code. In short, Visual-ERM provides a clearer, more reliable way to teach AI to "draw the right picture" by telling it exactly what to fix when its renderings diverge from the target.

summary: This paper introduced Visual-ERM, a multimodal reward model that provides fine-grained, interpretable feedback to evaluate vision-to-code quality directly in the rendered visuals, which when integrated into reinforcement learning improves chart-to-code, table, and SVG parsing, and introduces VC-RewardBench for evaluating visual discrepancies, becoming the foundation for robust vision-to-code RL applications.
